questions.question: The fifth plague was a disease that struck Egypt's ______.

candidates.text: Egypt deified its herds, the Apis bull and the cow-goddess Hathor. When the Lord strikes the livestock He is not killing random cattle; He is executing judgment on the gods of Egypt, the very beasts they bowed to. Plague five is a divine raid on Egypt's barn full of idols.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

candidates.text: Plague five hits Egypt's barn, and Scripture later names the bigger point: God was judging Egypt's gods. Remember it as a barnyard takedown—livestock falls, idols lose, Israel's herds live.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 9:3-6 says the fifth plague struck Egypt's livestock but not Israel's, and Exodus 12:12 says the Lord executed judgment against Egypt's gods.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Historical gods claim needs a public supporting source.

questions.question: Which of the following animals was NOT named among those struck by the fifth plague?

candidates.text: The named five in order: Horses, Donkeys, Camels, Oxen, Sheep, 'Happy Donkeys Carry Old Sheep.' The goat alone is absent from the list; let its absence be your answer.

questions.question: Israel's livestock died along with the livestock of Egypt.

candidates.text: Notice the verb Exodus uses: the Lord 'separates' Israel's cattle from Egypt's, the same separating He did at creation between light and dark. Not one beast of Israel died though every Egyptian herd was struck. Goshen becomes a pocket of the new creation inside the old, the line of the elect drawn already before Passover marks it in blood.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God

candidates.text: God draws a line before Passover ever arrives: Egypt's herds die, Israel's do not. Picture a divine boundary fence around Goshen.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 9:4-6 says the Lord made a difference between Egyptian and Israelite livestock, and not one of Israel's animals died.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Creation and elect typology are not source-auditable as written.

questions.question: What did Moses toss into the air to bring about the sixth plague?

candidates.text: Watch Moses fling a double handful of furnace ashes toward heaven, the soot of the very kiln where Hebrew slaves baked Pharaoh's bricks. The gray dust drifts down over all the land and blooms into festering boils on man and beast. The instrument of Israel's slavery rains back as judgment on the slavemasters.
Moses throws furnace ashes like gray confetti; when the dust lands, Egypt breaks out in boils. Ashes up, sores out.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Brick-kiln reversal is vivid but not supported here with a source.

questions.question: In the sixth plague, Moses' furnace ashes caused ______ that broke out in sores on both man and beast.

candidates.text: Ashes up, boils down: the furnace soot Moses flings skyward settles as boils that break open into sores (LXX 'sore blains') — and they fall on man AND beast alike. Boils swell; sores break.
Ashes up, boils pop: Moses throws furnace dust, and skin erupts. Up goes ash; out come boils.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; The LXX note muddies a good mnemonic and adds an unsupported detail.

questions.question: During the sixth plague the magicians could not stand before Moses, because the sores had broken out on them as well.

candidates.text: Verse 11 turns the knife: the sorcerers who matched Moses' early signs cannot even stand before him now, for the sores were on the magicians themselves. Egypt's supposed mediators of the gods are exposed as powerless flesh, erupting with the same boils as every slave and beast.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Egypt's magicians copied early signs, but boils bench them. By plague six they cannot even stand before Moses.
candidates.sourceClaim: These passages show Egypt's magicians imitated earlier signs, but Exodus 9:11 says they could not stand before Moses because of the boils.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good reversal, but the interpretive framing needs a source or trimming.

questions.question: What unusual thing flashed within the hail of the seventh plague?

candidates.text: See ice and fire falling as one: flaming hail streaking down while fire runs along the ground. A sky divided against itself, ice and flame agreeing only to destroy.

questions.question: Which two crops were destroyed by the hail?

candidates.text: 'Flax and Barley Bit the dust' -- the early B-crop and the flax were ripe and exposed, so the hail shattered them. Wheat and spelt came later and lived; ripeness is what got them killed.
F and B got flattened: Flax and Barley. When hail hits, remember the two battered crops by saying, 'Flax broken, barley broken.'
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; The “early B-crop” wording weakens an otherwise useful memory hook.

questions.question: Which two crops survived the hail because they ripen later?

candidates.text: 'Wheat and Spelt came Late, so they Wait.' Not yet risen above the ground, the latecomers were hidden from the hail; what had not grown up could not be cut down.

questions.question: The wheat and spelt survived the hail.

candidates.text: 'Late equals safe.' Wheat and spelt ripen later and had not yet grown up, so the hail passed them by. The unripened crops survived.

questions.question: Everyone in Egypt lost their servants and animals to the hail.

candidates.text: This is the first plague where Scripture records Egyptians choosing sides: whoever feared the word of the Lord drove his servants and cattle under shelter and was spared. Even among Pharaoh's officials the fear of the Lord creates a remnant. The line of salvation is drawn by faith, not by ethnicity, well before Passover.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Some Egyptians actually listened. During the hail warning, fear ran indoors and pride stayed in the field.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 9:20-21 says some of Pharaoh's servants feared the word of the Lord and sheltered their servants and livestock, while others left them in the field.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The core point is scriptural, but the remnant-by-faith framing needs direct support.

questions.question: During the hail, Pharaoh confessed, 'I have ______ this time.'

candidates.text: Hear Pharaoh cry 'I have sinned!' over the roar of the thunder, then watch his face harden the instant the hail stops. A confession molded out of ice, melting the moment the danger passes.

questions.question: Pharaoh truly repented after confessing his sin during the hail.

candidates.text: Pharaoh confessed with his mouth while his heart stayed stone, and the moment the hail ceased he sinned again. This is the sorrow of the world that fears the lash but not the Lord; godly grief changes the man, but worldly regret only wants the storm to stop.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Pharaoh says, 'I have sinned,' then sins again as soon as the hail stops. Mouth-confession, hard heart—that is why the statement is False.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 9:27 records Pharaoh saying he sinned, and Exodus 9:34-35 says he sinned again and hardened his heart after the hail stopped.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Worldly-sorrow contrast is useful but needs explicit source support.

questions.question: The eighth plague brought a swarm of ______ upon Egypt.

candidates.text: Eight = ATE. The eighth plague is the plague that ate, the locusts devouring everything the hail had spared. Say 'eight ate' and you cannot forget it.

questions.question: Which wind did God use to bring the locusts upon Egypt?

candidates.text: The east wind is the Lord's war-wind, the same east wind that will split the sea two chapters later. East brings the locusts to feast; east drives back the waters to deliver. The breath of God is the weapon throughout Exodus.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

candidates.text: East starts both Exodus wind scenes: east brings locusts here, and east later drives back the sea. For this question, think, 'East = insects enter.'
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 10:13 says an east wind brought the locusts, and Exodus 14:21 says a strong east wind drove back the sea.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Strong cross-reference, but it needs a real supporting source.

questions.question: The locusts ate everything that was left after the hail.

candidates.text: See the very wheat and spelt that survived the hail now stripped to bare stalks. A living green tide of insects pours over the land and swallows the last harvest Egypt had left; nothing green remained.

questions.question: Pharaoh's servants asked, 'How long shall this man be a ______ to us?'

candidates.text: See Pharaoh's own court turn and point at Moses: 'How long shall this man be a snare to us?' His own servants see the trap closing before the king will admit it.

questions.question: During the locust plague, Pharaoh offered to let everyone in Israel leave.

candidates.text: Pharaoh would loose only the men and hold the little ones back as a pledge of return. This is ever the enemy's bargain, to grant a partial freedom that he may keep his grip; a worship that leaves your family in Egypt is exactly the deal the deceiver wants you to take.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Pharaoh offers a men-only exit and tries to keep the families in Egypt. Men may go; everyone does not—so the answer is False.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The enemy/deceiver application is not source-backed and goes beyond the quiz fact.

questions.question: The ninth plague covered Egypt in thick ______.

candidates.text: Nine = Night. The ninth plague turned Egypt into one unbroken night for three days; let the rhyme of nine and night hold the number fast.

questions.question: How long did the darkness cover the land of Egypt?

candidates.text: Three days of darkness, and the number rhymes forward: three days in the belly of the fish, three days in the tomb. Egypt is buried in a darkness it can feel for the very span of death before resurrection. Darkness for THREE.
Three dark days: count them on three fingers in a blackout. Egypt's lights stay out for THREE.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Jonah and tomb typology need sources; the quiz only needs the duration.

questions.question: The Israelites also sat in darkness like the Egyptians.

candidates.text: Egypt sat in darkness that could be felt, yet all Israel had light in their dwellings. This is the original division of the children of light from the children of darkness; the selfsame Presence that is blinding gloom to Egypt is warmth and light to those who abide with Him.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Egypt sat in blackout, but Israel had light at home. Remember the contrast as 'dark in Egypt, lamps in Goshen.'
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 10:22-23 says thick darkness covered Egypt for three days, but the children of Israel had light in their dwellings.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The light-versus-darkness contrast is strong, but the theological overlay needs support.

questions.question: After the darkness, Pharaoh offered to let everyone go except their livestock.

candidates.text: Watch Pharaoh haggle like a man who refuses to release his hostages: first 'only the men may go,' now 'everyone — but the flocks stay behind.' Every concession still clutches something back; this time the livestock are the chain he hopes will drag Israel home.

questions.question: Moses replied that not one ______ of their livestock would be left behind.

candidates.text: See Moses plant his staff: 'Not one hoof stays behind!' Every beast is needed for sacrifice to the Lord; God's people depart whole, leaving nothing of themselves in Egypt's hand.
Picture Moses checking the barn door: not one hoof—not even a toenail—stays in Egypt. Hoof means every animal goes.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_rewrite; The final sentence drifts into application beyond the quiz fact.

questions.question: After the darkness, one final plague still remained for Egypt.

candidates.text: Nine plagues have been escalating skirmishes; the tenth is the killing blow aimed at the heir. In the ancient world the firstborn son is the future, the inheritance, the dynasty, so the final plague does not just wound Egypt, it ends Egypt's tomorrow. One blow left, and it lands on the throne's successor.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

candidates.text: After nine plagues, God says one more is left. The last blow is the death of the firstborn, so remember: darkness was not the end.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 11:1 says the Lord would bring one more plague on Egypt, and Exodus 11:5 identifies it as the death of the firstborn.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The dynasty explanation is plausible but not source-auditable as written.

questions.question: God declared that about ______ He would go out through the midst of Egypt.

candidates.text: Fix the hour at midnight, the deepest point of the night, when the Lord Himself goes out through the midst of Egypt. He walks straight into death's own hour and turns it into the hour of deliverance, the same midnight at which the Bridegroom comes and the dead will rise.
Set the clock to 12:00. At midnight the Lord goes through Egypt—deep night, exact hour, unforgettable word.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Bridegroom and resurrection typology need source support and distract from the key word.

questions.question: Israel was told to leave Egypt immediately, before the final plague struck.

candidates.text: Israel did not bolt in panic; they waited, and what they waited for was a liturgy. Before the Exodus there must be the Passover meal, given in the very next chapter; deliverance comes through worship, not flight. God feeds His people on the Lamb at the altar before He leads them out the door.
candidates.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

candidates.text: Do not picture Israel sprinting out in Exodus 11. Picture a giant PAUSE button: one more plague is announced, then Exodus 12 gives the Passover before the exit.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 11 announces one more plague, and Exodus 12 gives the Passover instructions before the plague occurs and before Israel leaves Egypt.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Vivid, but the liturgy/altar claims are not auditable from the current source fields.

questions.question: Israel was promised they would leave Egypt carrying what?

candidates.text: This is not looting but back-wages, and deeper, the spoils of a defeated army. The Divine Warrior has conquered Egypt and His people carry off the plunder, silver and gold that will become the gold of the tabernacle. Egypt's treasure is conscripted into the worship of the God who beat its gods.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

candidates.text: Picture the Israelites walking out with shiny exit tickets: SILVER and GOLD. Not empty-handed—jingling-handed.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 12:35-36 says Israel asked the Egyptians for articles of silver and gold and received them.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; Strong imagery, but the back-wages/tabernacle/gods claims need auditable support.

questions.question: What was required of the Passover lamb's condition?

candidates.text: A lamb without blemish, because only the flawless can stand in the place of the firstborn. Melito saw it plainly: the spotless lamb is the type of the spotless Christ, the only one who can offer Himself for the life of all. The blemish test is a christology lesson.
No spots, no shortcuts: the lamb had to be “without blemish.” St. Peter later calls Christ a lamb “without blemish and without spot,” so the phrase sticks: spotless lamb, spotless Christ.
candidates.sourceClaim: 1 Peter 1:18-19 calls Christ a lamb without blemish and without spot.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The spotless-lamb connection is memorable, but the current patristic source is not auditable here.

questions.question: What sex and age was the Passover lamb to be?

candidates.text: A MALE in his first YEAR, without blemish: 'a young male, in his prime, no flaw.' Three marks to keep: male, year-old, perfect.

questions.question: The Passover lamb was to be a female of the flock.

candidates.text: Not female but MALE, because the lamb dies in the place of the firstborn SON of the house. The substitution only works son-for-son; that is why John the Baptist points and says 'Behold the Lamb,' not a ewe. Male, year-old, unblemished.
Ewe? Ew—no. The Passover lamb was MALE: boy lamb, one year old, no blemish.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The son-for-son explanation is not supported by the cited source.

questions.question: On which day of the month was the Passover lamb to be set apart?

candidates.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Set apart on the 10th and slain on the 14th, four days of proving show the lamb is truly without blemish before it dies.
TEN tags the lamb; FOURTEEN is kill day.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; The rhyme sticks, but “inspect/perfect” may cloud the simple date to remember.

questions.question: On which day of the month was the Passover lamb to be killed?

candidates.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Chosen on the 10th, killed on the 14th between dusk and dark, when the days of inspection are done.
TEN gets the tag; FOURTEEN gets the fire. Kill day = 14.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; The wording adds extra ideas when the key fact is just the 14th day.

questions.question: Where was the blood of the Passover lamb to be applied?

candidates.text: Blood up the two side posts and across the top lintel: Justin Martyr already saw the doorway traced in blood as the shape of the Cross. The household is saved by passing under a bloodied cross-frame, centuries before Golgotha. The saving sign points up and out, never trampled below.
candidates.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 111

candidates.text: Paint a red goalpost on the door: two side posts and the top lintel. No blood on the floor.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The doorway-as-cross idea is not actually supported by the current source fields.

questions.question: The Passover blood was to be placed on the threshold of the door.

candidates.text: Side posts and lintel, never the threshold, because you will not tread the saving blood underfoot. The geometry itself is theology: the Cross is over you, never under your shoe; the blood of the Lamb is reverenced and lifted up, not stepped upon.
candidates.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 111

candidates.text: Top and sides, not the step: red goalpost, no red doormat. Doorposts and lintel—not threshold.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The theology is unsupported, and the row reference appears mismatched because Exodus 12:46 is not the blood-on-doorpost verse.

questions.question: Which three things made up the Passover meal?
questions.answer: Lamb roasted with fire, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs
options: Lamb roasted with fire, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs
Boiled lamb, leavened bread, and sweet herbs
Roasted lamb, unleavened bread, and honey
Raw lamb, unleavened bread, and bitter herbs

candidates.text: Three for the meal, 'Fire, Flat, and Bitter': lamb roasted by Fire, Flat unleavened bread, and Bitter herbs. The fire of the Passion, the sinless bread, the bitterness of bondage, all eaten in one night.
Fire, Flat, Bitter: fire-roasted lamb, flat unleavened bread, bitter herbs. Three bites, one night.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The core mnemonic is good, but the added Passion and symbolism claims need sources.

questions.question: How was the Passover lamb to be cooked?

candidates.text: Roasted whole over fire, never boiled, never raw. Justin records that the lamb was spitted on two crossed skewers, head-to-tail and across, so the very lamb on the fire hung in the shape of a cross. The cooking method is a crucifix, the Lamb wholly given over in His Passion.
candidates.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 111

candidates.text: Do not boil the lamb—picture it on fire. Justin Martyr says the roasted Passover lamb was arranged in the form of a cross, so “roasted with fire” sticks: the fiery lamb points to the Cross.
candidates.source: St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho, ch. 40
candidates.sourceClaim: Justin Martyr says the Passover lamb was wholly roasted as a symbol of Christ’s suffering on the cross and describes it as arranged in the form of a cross.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Great hook, but the Justin citation needs a correct auditable source.

questions.question: The Passover bread was to be leavened.

candidates.text: Unleavened, because leaven is the biblical image of corruption spreading through the lump, and there was no time to rise anyway. Paul makes it the Christian's command: keep the feast not with the old leaven of malice but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth, for Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us.
Leaven is the little puff that takes over the whole lump. Paul connects Passover to Christ and says to keep the feast with the “unleavened bread” of sincerity and truth—so the answer is no yeast.
candidates.sourceClaim: 1 Corinthians 5:6-8 says a little leaven leavens the whole lump, Christ our Passover was sacrificed, and the feast is kept with unleavened sincerity and truth.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; Good teaching, but it needs an auditable verse link and tighter wording.

questions.question: Along with the lamb and unleavened bread, the meal included bitter ______.

candidates.text: The bitter herbs are the taste of slavery, eaten beside the unleavened bread so the memory of bondage is never lost. You taste the bondage even as you celebrate the rescue; keep the feast, then, not with the old leaven of malice but with sincerity and truth, recalling the bitterness from which Christ our Passover has set us free.
Bitter blanks bite: lamb, flat bread, and bitter HERBS. If your tongue winces, the answer is herbs.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The bitterness-as-slavery explanation is not supported by the listed source.

questions.question: Of this feast God declared, 'It is the Lord's ______.'

candidates.text: Pascha means the 'passing over' of the destroyer, and the name itself is the gospel in one verb. Melito sees it fulfilled in Christ the slain Lamb, by whose own blood death passes over us and we pass over from death into life.
PASSOVER is the feast name because God passed over the blood-marked house. The blank is PASSOVER.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 12:27 explains the Passover as the Lord passing over Israel’s houses in Egypt when He struck the Egyptians.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The etymology and Christ-fulfillment claims are not auditable from the current source fields.

questions.question: Who died in the tenth and final plague?

candidates.text: Every firstborn, and Exodus tells you why: 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment.' Pharaoh was reckoned a living god whose divine line ran through his firstborn, so striking every firstborn topples the whole pantheon and its incarnate king in one night. From the palace to the prison, no house untouched but those under the blood.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

candidates.text: At midnight the FIRSTBORN stamp goes dark across Egypt—palace, prison, even livestock. The answer is not “some”; it is every firstborn in Egypt.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 12:29 says the Lord struck all the firstborn in Egypt, from Pharaoh’s firstborn to the captive’s firstborn and the firstborn of livestock.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The Pharaoh-and-pantheon explanation is vivid but not auditable from the current source fields.

questions.question: Who was spared from the death of the firstborn?

candidates.text: Only the blood on the door saved the house, not flight, not merit, not hiding. So too only the blood of Christ shelters us; the destroyer passes over all who are marked by the Lamb, a salvation given by grace through the sign of sacrifice.
Look for the red mark, not the address. God says that when He sees the blood, He will pass over—so the spared houses are the blood-covered houses.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 12:13 says the blood on the houses would be a sign, and when the Lord saw the blood He would pass over them.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=needs_source; The core idea is right, but the current teaching needs a direct auditable source.

questions.question: Pharaoh's own son died in the final plague.

candidates.text: Pharaoh's firstborn was the next living god of Egypt, the heir of the sun-god's throne. When he dies, the entire theology of Egyptian kingship dies with him in a single night; the god-king watches his own divine succession struck down. The hardest heart finally pays in the currency it valued most.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

candidates.text: Pharaoh’s palace was not plague-proof. Exodus names Pharaoh’s own firstborn in the midnight judgment—yes, his son too.
candidates.sourceClaim: Exodus 12:29 says the Lord struck Pharaoh’s firstborn along with all the firstborn in Egypt.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The Egyptian-kingship explanation needs a real source and goes beyond the verse.

questions.question: How many years did Israel sojourn in Egypt and Canaan?

candidates.text: 'Four-three-oh, time to go.' The Septuagint counts 430 years as the full span of sojourning in Egypt and Canaan together; on the very day they ended, the host of the Lord went out, deliverance arriving exactly on time.
Four-three-oh, time to go. Canaan plus Egypt on the scoreboard: 430.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; The Septuagint-text claim needs sourcing; the number can be remembered more simply.

questions.question: About how many men left Egypt, not counting women and children?

candidates.text: 'Six hundred thousand, and many more besides.' About 600,000 men on foot, and that is before a single woman or child is counted, a whole nation born in a night.

questions.question: The figure of about 600,000 leaving Egypt counted only the men, not the women and children.

candidates.text: The 600,000 counts only the fighting-age men, and that word matters, because Exodus calls this host the Lord's armies marching out in battle array. The census is a muster roll; what leaves Egypt is not just a people but an army, with the women and children filling in a whole nation behind the ranks.
candidates.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War

candidates.text: 600,000 is the men-only headline; families are still off-screen. Think: 600K men, plus women and children.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=unsupported_claim; “Fighting-age” and “muster roll” go beyond the verse and need support.

questions.question: The Passover lamb was set apart on the 10th day and killed on the 14th day of the month.

candidates.text: '10 to inspect, 14 to perfect.' Four days under inspection prove the lamb without blemish before it is slain; chosen on the 10th, killed on the 14th.
TEN gets the tag; FOURTEEN gets the fire. Set apart on 10, killed on 14.
candidates.styleNotes: agentVerdict=confusing; The rhyme is catchy, but “inspect/perfect” adds extra baggage to a date question.
